feedback.text: Hi. I've been playing games since an early age and I've experienced, first hand, the evolution of gaming from it being contained within physical media that cannot be deleted to it being something only available via online features. Sadly the previously promised positives there'd be to having a game available through online means (making sure the game works/bug fixes, improving the game by responding to user input from the players) have pretty much all fallen to the wayside, while the downsides (DLC (Downloadable Content (paid content), bans/restrictions from being able to play your game due to How you played it, even if it's a single player game, forcing advertisements into games via both in-game store popups as well as advertisements overtaking the game for around a minute in order to gain access to your game again) have slowly been taking root and increasing. I'd like to mention an example of how some games change the way they're played in order to make it easier for them to earn money. I play a phone game called Pokemon Masters, a "gacha" game (an infamous genre of what you could call gambling/lottery systems in order to earn something in the game, in most cases the different playable characters). This game started off with it being relatively kind towards people who were unwilling to pay, as there was no way to compete with other players in any way whatsoever while also having all characters be available for the currency you earn within the game. Over the years, however, this has changed. The game received a lot of negative feedback any time it attempted to coerce the players to accept PvP (Player vs Player) into the game. PvP is a great way to incentivize real money spending in these games, as the one who spends the most money will have the best characters, whereas those who don't spend at all will either have to suffer with always being weaker than those who do spend or simply quit. They have yet to add PvP into the game, but instead added ways to compete through Leaderboards: whoever obtains the most amount of points in an event (such as whoever performs the best versus a certain boss) wins the event, creating an incentive for spending. They've recently also added a Guild system (20man groups of players) where you're rewarded depending on how well your guild as a whole perform as well as your own performance. This creates a sort of group pressure, where you may feel worried that you'll lose your spot if you don't perform well enough, leading some people to spend simply to fit in. Another addition to the game is that around a year ago they added a new type of character that can Only be obtained through spending the currency only available via real money spending. These characters are (of course) very strong and thus are pretty much a necessary "investment" if you want to be among the top performers in the game. Games used to be about skill, creativity, strategy when it came to performance, something that even this game was relatively close to, even if only early on. When earning money becomes the main goal for a game it's easy to see how these implementations slowly creep into the game, while also having an infamous feature known as Power Creep (roughly speaking: all new additions to the game vastly outperform previous additions, meaning "if my current character was worth X amount of money then this one's even more worthwhile, because this one's even better!"). Games such as this are impossible for normal players to revert or change to be playable at the earlier stage, where the game was still enjoyable, but there are games that focus on releasing sequels rather than updating the current game. The prequels are sometimes considered better due to not having those negative sides to them, which often leads to the prequels being considered a competitor. A common resolution to this issue is to simply make the previous title impossible to play, which is something I'd like to ask your help to prevent. Thank you for your time.
